## Mixed-Effects Logistic Regression with Participant-Level Slopes

Building on the previous model, we next introduced a random slope for similarity difference ($\text{simDiff}$) at the participant level, while retaining the random intercept for character:

$$
\text{chosen} \;\sim\; \text{simDiff} \times \text{task\_type} \times \text{condition} \;+\; (1 \mid \text{character}) \;+\; \bigl(0 + \text{simDiff} \mid \text{subject}\bigr).
$$

This more complex specification allows each participant to differ in how strongly they weigh similarity, even as the model still adjusts for character-level variability. In contrast to the previous simpler model (AIC $\approx 7299$), the addition of random slopes substantially improved fit (AIC $\approx 6764$), suggesting meaningful person-to-person heterogeneity in the effect of $\text{simDiff}$.

### Fixed Effects
Under this model, the main effect of $\text{simDiff}$ remains positive ($\hat{\beta} \approx 0.40$, $p < 0.01$) in the baseline scenario (Pairwise Choice, Casual condition), indicating that a higher surplus of shared features substantially increases the log-odds of selecting the focal character. As before, the slope of $\text{simDiff}$ is greatly reduced in Full Ranking tasks ($\hat{\beta}_{\text{simDiff}:\text{full\_ranking}} \approx -0.48$, $p \ll 0.001$). The two-way interaction with Competitive framing is now smaller and not strictly significant ($p \approx 0.11$), while the three-way interaction ($\text{simDiff}:\text{full\_ranking}:\text{Competitive}$) remains highly significant ($\hat{\beta} \approx 0.50$, $p \ll 0.001$), partially offsetting the slope reductions when both factors coincide.

### Random Effects
- **Character Intercept**: The variance ($\sigma^2 \approx 1.06$) is comparable to the previous model, implying that certain characters are still chosen considerably more often (or less often) than others, regardless of participant or condition.
- **Participant Slopes for $\text{simDiff}$**: We now observe variance $\approx 0.48$ ($\text{Std. Dev.} \approx 0.70$). Some participants show a much steeper rise in choosing more-similar characters, while others weigh $\text{simDiff}$ more modestly or even negatively. This confirms substantial individual differences in sensitivity to shared features.

Overall, adding the random slope reveals that participants vary meaningfully in how strongly shared demographic features shape their choices, above and beyond the fixed-effect patterns of Task Type and Condition. This richer model thus offers both a better fit and deeper insight into the personal variability of homophily preferences.
